segments.from: European port of departure (Liverpool, Hamburg, Bremen, Naples, Trieste, Gothenburg)
segments.conditions: Steerage passengers were housed below the waterline in open compartments holding 200-300 people on tiered metal bunks with straw mattresses. Ventilation was minimal, seasickness was universal in the first days, and the stench of vomit, sweat, and bilge water was inescapable.

segments.to: New York Harbor — Castle Garden (pre-1892) / Ellis Island (1892-1924)
segments.cost: included in ticket; additional fees of $1-$2 for head tax at port of entry
segments.conditions: As the ship approached New York, steerage passengers were brought on deck to see the Statue of Liberty — first- and second-class passengers had already been cleared aboard ship by inspectors, but steerage travelers were ferried to Ellis Island for medical examination, legal inspection, and the possibility of deportation that affected roughly 2% of arrivals.

who_used_it: German and Scandinavian immigrants (1855-1890s), followed by Southern and Eastern European immigrants — Italians, Poles, Russians, Hungarians, Greeks, and Eastern European Jews — in the peak years of 1880-1914. Also Chinese immigrants on Pacific crossings to Angel Island used similar steerage conditions.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely crossed the Atlantic in steerage class from {origin}, spending 10 to 14 days below decks in conditions that one immigration inspector described as 'unfit for cattle,' before arriving at {destination} to face inspection and the uncertain first hours in America.
source_references: Maldwyn Allen Jones, 'American Immigration' (University of Chicago Press, 1960)
John Bodnar, 'The Transplanted: A History of Immigrants in Urban America' (Indiana University Press, 1985)
Edward Steiner, 'On the Trail of the Immigrant' (Fleming H. Revell, 1906)
Alan M. Kraut, 'Silent Travelers: Germs, Genes, and the Immigrant Menace' (Johns Hopkins University Press, 1994)
